docs.title: Django CeleryX
docs.text: Celery monitoring and management for Django admin. Like Flower, but embedded in your Django admin with htmx + Alpine.js for real-time updates.

docs.title: Features
docs.text:
• Real-time task monitoring — Live task list with state, args, result, timing
  • Worker management — View worker status, pool info, queues, config
  • Control actions — Revoke/terminate tasks, shutdown/restart workers, manage pools and queues
  • Metrics dashboard — Throughput, latency percentiles, success/failure rates
  • Broker overview — Queue names and message counts
  • Django admin native — Looks and feels like Django admin, no separate service
  • No npm/build step — htmx + Alpine.js included as static files

docs.title: Quick Start
docs.text:
INSTALLED_APPS = [
    # ...
    "django_celeryx.admin",
]

That's it. Start your Django server and navigate to the admin to see your Celery tasks and workers.

docs.title: Requirements
docs.text:
• Python 3.12+
  • Django 5.2+
  • Celery 5.4+

docs.title: Install
docs.text:
pip install django-celeryx

For django-unfold themed admin:

pip install django-celeryx[unfold]

docs.title: Setup
docs.text:
Add django_celeryx.admin to your INSTALLED_APPS:

INSTALLED_APPS = [
    # ...
    "django_celeryx.admin",
]

For django-unfold, use django_celeryx.unfold instead.

docs.title: Basic Setup
docs.text:
1. Install django-celeryx:
pip install django-celeryx[celery]
  1. Add to your Django settings:
INSTALLED_APPS = [
    # ...
    "django_celeryx.admin",
]
  1. Start your Django server and navigate to the admin. You'll see Tasks, Workers, Queues, and Registered Tasks sections.

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
Optional configuration via CELERYX dict in settings:

CELERYX = {
    "MAX_TASK_COUNT": 100_000,
    "MAX_TASK_AGE": 86400,
    "AUTO_REFRESH_INTERVAL": 3,
}

See Configuration for all options.

docs.title: API Reference
docs.text: ::: django_celeryx

docs.title: 0.1.0 (Unreleased)
docs.text:
• Initial release
  • Event-driven task and worker monitoring
  • Django admin integration with unmanaged models
  • Real-time UI with htmx + idiomorph
  • Worker control actions
  • Metrics dashboard

docs.title: Broker
docs.text: The broker view shows queue information from connected workers.

docs.title: Queue List
docs.text:
• Queue name
  • Exchange name
  • Routing key
  • Consumer count (number of workers consuming from this queue)

Queue data is fetched via celery.control.inspect().active_queues().

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
All settings are optional and configured via the CELERYX dict in your Django settings.

CELERYX = {
    # Celery app (dotted path). Auto-detected if None.
    "CELERY_APP": None,

    # Database alias for storing task/worker state.
    # None = auto-configured dedicated SQLite file (celeryx.sqlite3).
    # Set to a DATABASES alias to use your own database (e.g. "default", "celeryx").
    "DATABASE": None,

    # Maximum age of stored task records in seconds (default 24h).
    "MAX_TASK_AGE": 86400,

    # Maximum number of task records in the database.
    # Oldest records are pruned when this limit is exceeded.
    "MAX_TASK_COUNT": 100_000,

    # Set to False to disable monitoring on this instance.
    # Useful in multi-pod deployments where only one pod should monitor.
    "ADMIN_ENABLED": True,

    # Start the event listener automatically on Django startup.
    "EVENT_LISTENER_AUTOSTART": True,

    # Periodically broadcast enable_events to workers.
    "ENABLE_EVENTS": True,

    # Timeout for celery.control.inspect() calls.
    "INSPECT_TIMEOUT": 1.0,

    # Seconds between htmx live update polls (0 to disable).
    "AUTO_REFRESH_INTERVAL": 3,

    # Visible columns on the task list page.
    "TASK_COLUMNS": [
        "name", "uuid", "state", "worker", "received", "started", "runtime",
    ],

    # Use relative timestamps (e.g. "5 minutes ago") instead of absolute.
    "NATURAL_TIME": False,

    # Prometheus metric name prefix. Set to "flower" for Flower drop-in compat.
    "PROMETHEUS_PREFIX": "django_celeryx",
}

docs.title: Database Persistence
docs.text:
By default, django-celeryx stores all state in an auto-configured SQLite file database (celeryx.sqlite3) placed alongside your default database. To use a different database backend:

# Option 1: Use an explicit database
DATABASES = {
    "default": { ... },
    "celeryx": {
        "ENGINE": "django.db.backends.sqlite3",
        "NAME": BASE_DIR / "celeryx.sqlite3",
    },
}

CELERYX = {
    "DATABASE": "celeryx",
}

# Required: add the database router
DATABASE_ROUTERS = ["django_celeryx.db_router.CeleryXRouter"]

Then run migrations:

python manage.py migrate --database=celeryx

docs.title: Multi-Pod Deployments
docs.text:
In production, you typically run multiple Django instances. Only one should run the event listener:

# On the monitoring pod:
CELERYX = {
    "ADMIN_ENABLED": True,
    "DATABASE": "celeryx",
}

# On all other pods:
CELERYX = {
    "ADMIN_ENABLED": False,
}

docs.title: Dashboard
docs.text: The metrics dashboard provides an overview of your Celery cluster's performance.

docs.title: Charts
docs.text:
• Throughput — Succeeded/failed tasks per time bin (Chart.js)
  • Slowest tasks — Average runtime with standard deviation by task type
  • Failure rate — Failure percentage by task type
  • Worker load — Task counts per worker
  • Top tasks — Most frequently executed task types

docs.title: Time Periods
docs.text:
Filter the dashboard by time period:

  • Today — Last 24 hours
  • Last 7 days
  • Last 30 days

docs.title: Filters
docs.text:
• Queue — filter by routing key
  • Worker — filter by worker hostname

docs.title: Tasks
docs.text: The task list provides a real-time view of all Celery tasks.

docs.title: Task List
docs.text:
Auto-refreshing table showing tasks with configurable columns:

  • name, uuid, state, args, kwargs, result
  • received, started, runtime
  • worker, exchange, routing_key
  • retries, revoked, exception, expires, eta

docs.title: Task States
docs.text: Color-coded states: PENDING, RECEIVED, STARTED, SUCCESS, FAILURE, RETRY, REVOKED.

docs.title: Search
docs.text:
Structured search with prefix support:

  • Free text across all fields
  • state:FAILURE — Filter by state
  • result:value — Search results
  • args:value — Search arguments
  • kwargs:key=value — Search keyword arguments

docs.title: Actions
docs.text:
• Revoke — Cancel a pending task
  • Terminate — Kill a running task
  • Abort — Abort an abortable task

docs.title: Workers
docs.text: The worker list shows all active Celery workers with their status.

docs.title: Worker Detail Tabs
docs.text:
• Pool — Pool type, concurrency, processes. Controls: grow/shrink, autoscale
  • Queues — Active queues. Controls: add/cancel consumer
  • Tasks — Processed counts, active/scheduled/reserved/revoked tasks
  • Limits — Rate limits and timeouts. Controls: set rate limit, set timeout
  • Config — Full worker Celery configuration
  • Stats — System resource usage, broker connection info

docs.title: Control Actions
docs.text:
• Shutdown worker
  • Restart worker pool
  • Grow/shrink pool
  • Set autoscale limits
  • Add/cancel queue consumer
  • Set rate limits and time limits
